1: ḥaṭṭāʾt Yĕhûdâ kĕtûbâ bĕʿēt barzel bĕṣippōren šāmîr — "Judah's sin is written with an iron stylus; with a diamond tip it is engraved on the tablet of their heart"; lûaḥ libbām (the tablet of their heart) directly anticipates the new covenant promise: Jeremiah 31:33 promises the law written on the heart as blessing; here the sin is already written on the heart as curse. The MKT preserves the double inscription: on the heart (lûaḥ libbām) and on the horns of the altars (qarnôt mizbĕḥôtêkem).
2: kĕzĕkor bĕnêhem mizbĕḥôtām — "even their children remember their altars and their Asherah poles"; the intergenerational transmission of idolatry — the same cultural-transmission concern as Jeremiah 9:14. Children who remember the pagan cult sites means the practice is being taught as heritage, not abandoned as sin. "Every spreading tree on every high hill" — the stereotyped formula for hilltop-shrine Baal worship.
3: harî baśśādeh ḥêlĕkā kol-ʾôtsĕrôtêkā — "O mountain in the open country, I will hand over your wealth and all your treasures as plunder"; the "mountain" may refer to Jerusalem (Zion). The high shrines (bāmôtêkā) become the specific cause of the plunder: the cultic sites are both the site of sin and what triggers the punishment.
4: ûšāmaṭtā ûĕʾanāḥaltĕkā ʾăšer nātattî lāk — "through your own actions you will forfeit the inheritance I gave you"; šāmaṭ in the niphal means to release, let drop — the inheritance slips away through the people's own apostasy. "I will make you serve your enemies in a land you do not know" — the reversal from service of YHWH to service of enemies.
5: ʾārûr haggeber ʾăšer yibṭaḥ bāʾādām — "Cursed is the one who puts his trust in human beings"; the curse-formula (ʾārûr) matches the blessing-formula of Jeremiah 17:7 (bārûk). Bāṭaḥ bāʾādām (trusts in humanity) vs. bāṭaḥ baYHWH (trusts in YHWH) — the same verb exposes the choice. The MKT's "who turns his heart from the LORD" follows MT: wĕšāb libbô mēʾet YHWH.
6: kĕʿarôʿêr bāʿărābâ — "like a desert shrub" (ʿarôʿêr = juniper/tamarisk in the desert); the cursed person's condition: dwelling in parched places (ḥărērîm bammidbar), unable to benefit from good when it comes. The contrast with Jeremiah 17:8's tree-by-water will be stark. The MKT follows the tree/shrub imagery that connects directly to Psalm 1.
7: bārûk haggeber ʾăšer yibṭaḥ baYHWH — "Blessed is the one who trusts in the LORD"; the beatitude formula that mirrors Psalms 1:1-3 almost verbatim. The parallel structure (cursed/Jeremiah 17:5-6 :: blessed/Jeremiah 17:7-8) is the two-ways teaching at its most concentrated. Ûhāyâ YHWH mibṭaḥô — the LORD is his security (mibṭāḥ = place of trust/security).
8: kĕʿēts šātûl ʿal-mayim — "like a tree planted beside a stream"; the parallel with Psalms 1:3 is very close (kĕʿēts šātûl ʿal-palgê mayim). The tree's roots reach the water source (ʿal-yûbal yĕšallaḥ šorāšāyw). The phrase "it does not fear when the heat comes" (wĕlōʾ yirʾeh kî-yābôʾ ḥōm) — security through deep rootedness rather than superficial circumstances.
9: ʿāqōb hallēb mikkōl ûʾānûš hûʾ — "The heart is more deceitful than anything else and is desperately sick"; ʿāqōb (deceitful, treacherous) shares the root with Jacob's name (yaʿăqōb) — the heart that supplants and deceives. ʾĀnûš (incurable, desperately sick, weak) — the word used for incurable wounds (cf. Jeremiah 15:18). The rhetorical question mî yēdāʿennû (who can understand it?) is answered in Jeremiah 17:10.
10: ʾănî YHWH ḥōqēr lēb bōḥēn kĕlāyôt — "I, the LORD, examine the heart and test the inner mind (kĕlāyôt, kidneys/inner being)"; YHWH as the only adequate diagnostician of the human heart. Kĕlāyôt (kidneys/reins) was considered the seat of the deepest inner life, emotions, and will in Hebrew anthropology. The MKT's "inner mind" represents an attempt to convey what "kidneys" meant to ancient Israelites. The purpose: to give everyone what their conduct deserves — the heart-examiner is the perfectly just rewarder.
11: kĕqōrēʾ dāgar wĕlōʾ yālād — "like a partridge brooding over eggs she did not lay"; the qōrēʾ partridge was believed to steal and incubate other birds' eggs (the accuracy of this natural history is debated, but the proverb was current). The image: ill-gotten wealth is like stolen eggs — it hatches wealth that abandons its owner. The MKT preserves the proverb rather than explaining it away.
12: kissēʾ kābôd mārôm mĕrōšōn — "A glorious throne, exalted from the beginning" — a sudden praise-hymn for YHWH's heavenly throne. The juxtaposition with Jeremiah 17:11 (the partridge who loses his wealth) and Jeremiah 17:13 (those who forsake YHWH written in the dust) frames the throne-verse as a theological anchor: YHWH's throne is the fixed point against which all human pretension is measured.
13: YHWH miqweh Yiśrāʾēl — "O LORD, the hope of Israel"; the miqweh-title from 14:8 appears again — hope and water-reservoir. "Those who turn from you will have their names written in the dust" (yikkātĕbû bāʾārets) — the earth/dust as the anti-book: names written in dust versus names written in the book of life. The contrast: water-reservoir abandoned for broken cisterns leads to names written in dust rather than kept alive.
14: rĕpāʾênî YHWH wĕʾērāpēʾ — "Heal me, O LORD, and I will truly be healed"; the double-rāpāʾ (heal) construction — the source of healing and the healed state are both from YHWH. Similarly "save me, and I will truly be saved" (hôšîʿēnî wĕʾiwwāšēʿâ). The MKT's "truly" captures the emphasis that comes from the doubled verbal root: real/final healing depends entirely on YHWH's action.
15: ʾayyēh dĕbar-YHWH yābōʾ-nāʾ — "Where is the word of the LORD? Let it come, if it is real!" The taunt against Jeremiah: his prophecy of doom has not yet materialized. This is the standard challenge to the prophet: your announcement seems empty because the disaster has not arrived. The MKT's "if it is real" renders the skeptical nāʾ particle in context.
16: wĕʾănî lōʾ-ʾatsti mērōʿeh ʾaḥărêkā — "But I have not run away from the calling of being your shepherd"; rōʿeh (shepherd) here applied to Jeremiah as a pastor/shepherd figure following YHWH. He has not avoided (ʾûts min-) the painful calling. The MKT's "the calling of being your shepherd" is an interpretive rendering of the difficult phrase.
17: ʾal-tĕhî lî lĕmĕḥittâ — "Do not be a source of dread to me"; mĕḥittâ is terror, dread, dismay. Jeremiah asks that YHWH himself not become the source of his terror — a prayer that the divine presence remain as shelter (maḥseh) rather than threat. The MKT renders "you are my shelter in the day of disaster" following the positive formulation.
18: yēbōšû rōdĕpay wĕʾal-ʾēbōšâ ʾānî — "Let those who persecute me be shamed — but not me"; Jeremiah's imprecation against his persecutors. The threefold structure: shame/dismay/disaster — all the elements he fears for himself he asks YHWH to redirect onto his enemies. The MKT preserves the first-person singular throughout to maintain the intensity of the personal prayer.
19: šaʿar bĕnê-ʿām — "the People's Gate"; the location and the audience (malkê Yĕhûdâ, kings of Judah; kol-Yĕhûdâ, all Judah; yōšĕbê Yĕrûšālaim, inhabitants of Jerusalem) are given in full. The public proclamation at a major gate mirrors the temple sermon of Jeremiah 7 in its public setting.
20: Jeremiah is commissioned to call kings, Judah, and Jerusalem to hear the word of YHWH — the full public address formula. The inclusivity of the audience (kings to common people) emphasizes that the Sabbath command applies to all social levels equally.
21: šimĕrû napsôtêkem wĕʾal-tiśśĕʾû massāʾ bĕyôm haššabbāt — "Take care for your own sakes and do not carry any load on the Sabbath day"; šāmar nepeš (take care of yourself) implies the Sabbath prohibition is a matter of self-preservation, not merely legal compliance. The MKT's "take care for your own sakes" captures the reflexive force.
22: wĕlōʾ-tōṣîʾû massāʾ mibattêkem bĕyôm haššabbāt — "Do not carry burdens out of your houses on the Sabbath day"; the prohibition applies to outward commerce as well as inward carrying. "Keep the Sabbath day holy as I commanded your ancestors" (wĕqiddaštĕm ʾet-yôm haššabbāt kaʾăšer ṣiwwîtî ʾet-ʾăbôtêkem) — the ancestral command provides the authority for the current restatement.
23: wĕlōʾ šāmĕʿû wĕlōʾ hiṭṭû ʾet-ʾoznām — "But they did not listen or pay attention"; the standard refrain. Wayyaqšû ʾet-ʿarpām (stiffened their neck) — the qāšeh ʿōrep motif again (cf. Jeremiah 7:26). The pattern: instruction given → stiff-necked refusal → judgment. The historical precedent provides the context for the current conditional warnings.
24: The positive conditional: wĕhāyâ ʾim-šāmōaʿ tišmĕʿûn — the emphatic conditional ("if you truly listen to me"; infinitive absolute). If Sabbath is kept, the Davidic throne is preserved. The conditional structure mirrors the temple sermon (Jeremiah 7:3-7): the covenant's blessing depends on covenant-keeping. MKT preserves the "truly" construction.
25: ûbāʾû bĕšaʿărê hāʿîr hazzōʾt mĕlākim wĕśārîm — "then kings and princes sitting on David's throne will enter through the gates" — the restoration of the full Davidic political order is the positive consequence of Sabbath-keeping. The image of royal procession through the city gates (rōkĕbîm bārékeb ûvassûsîm) represents the full covenantal blessing in political form.
26: The geographic breadth of those who will come — Benjamin, Shephelah, Negeb, and Jerusalem itself — and the range of offerings — burnt offerings (ʿōlôt), sacrifices (zĕbāḥîm), grain offerings (minḥâ), frankincense (lĕbônâ), thank offerings (tôdâ) — represent the full restored worship of the covenant community. The MKT's list follows MT precisely.
27: wĕʾim-lōʾ tišmĕʿû ʾēlay lĕqaddēš ʾet-yôm haššabbāt — "But if you will not listen to me — if you refuse to keep the Sabbath day holy"; the negative conditional completes the two-ways structure. The fire that will burn Jerusalem's fortresses (wĕhiṣṣattî ʾēš bĕšĕʿārêhā) is the covenant curse. The MKT preserves the literal "fire into her gates" as the image of total destruction — fire at the gates is the final judgment on the city.
